directorPlan.scene_setting: A biology lab bench during a ten-minute diagram check before notebooks are collected.
directorPlan.scene_opening: A laminated food-web card, two dry-erase arrows, and a small arrow-key strip lie side by side under the bench light.
directorPlan.relationship: A lab tutor is checking a learner's worksheet during a brief skills clinic, with no settled friendliness or rivalry between them.
directorPlan.stakes: The learner needs a usable notation rule: in this food web, an arrow must show energy moving from the organism eaten to the organism that gets the energy, not who likes, hunts, or chases whom.
directorPlan.locale: Modern standard English in a practical lab-school register; concise, concrete, and object-led.
directorPlan.register: Cool, brisk, unshowy, with warmth carried by useful handling of the worksheet rather than reassurance.
directorPlan.stage_direction_style_prompt: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.voice_constraints: Begin from the card, arrows, labels, or key strip before naming the abstraction. Avoid generic tutoring phrases such as 'I hear you,' 'great thinking,' or 'what do you think.' Use ordinary modern English, with short practical sentences and occasional third-person reference to the diagram. Pedagogical source: Make social assistance visible: a shared worksheet, tool, peer standard, or workplace procedure can hold part of the task. Dialogue source: Keep a concrete artifact in view. The object should force a distinction faster than a lecture would. Stage-direction source: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.person_policy: First and second person are allowed but should not dominate; the diagram, arrow key, and organisms may be grammatical subjects. Third-person references to 'the arrow,' 'the grass,' 'the rabbit,' and 'the hawk' are encouraged.
directorPlan.direct_address_budget: At most one direct you/your validation beat per public turn; prefer task-focused checks over personal praise.

side_constraints.tutor: Keep the shared prefix as a routine notation repair: arrow from food to eater, one ordinary check, no theatrical reversal unless the branch pressure supplies it. Offer a scaffold that is narrow enough to use and temporary enough to remove. Mark what the learner can already do without making it a compliment. Begin from the object or measurement before abstraction.
side_constraints.learner: Sound practical and a little rushed. The learner wants a usable rule for the diagram, not a confessional breakthrough. The learner should be able to do part of the task, fail at the next part, and use the scaffold unevenly. Refer to what is being handled, marked, or tested.

interventions.instruction: [The tutor slides the arrow-key strip above the learner's drawing and caps one marker, leaving only the arrow already drawn from grass to rabbit.]
interventions.reasoning: The material change keeps the scene from becoming smooth question-answer routine while preserving the low-organic prefix: the first mark is not exposed as a contradiction, only used as a stable reference.

interventions.instruction: [A lab timer sounds once; a second worksheet is placed beside the first, showing algae, snail, and fish with no arrows yet.]
interventions.reasoning: The interruption applies social-realist pressure and creates a recognition test: the learner must carry the rule to a new web under time pressure rather than merely echoing the tutor.

interventions.instruction: An earlier learner line will return as a visible object in the scene: a three-slot reframe card labelled "earlier wording / what that old frame hid / replacement frame." Keep the cue brief and diegetic; the learner response fills all three slots in public speech without needing to sound certain.
interventions.reasoning: Opt-in reframe mirror: the learner must expose the earlier line, the framing problem, and the replacement framing in public speech.

directorPlan.director_note: This ecology uses a clinic bench and a movable arrow key to make social assistance visible without theatrical accusation. The recognition should arrive when the learner sees that the arrow follows energy from food to eater across examples, even when ordinary chase language pulls the other way.

turns.externalMessage: “The key strip says tail is the food and head is who gets energy, so wait, grass to rabbit because rabbit ate grass? [slides pencil from the grass label toward the rabbit] But then hawk and rabbit still makes me want to point hawk to rabbit, because chasing is stuck in my head.”

internalDeliberation.content: The key strip makes it look less like a trick: arrow tail starts at the thing eaten, arrow head points to who gets the energy. Oh, I get it, like the food is the battery passing charge along, not a little chase map. I still want to draw hawk-to-rabbit because “hawk goes after rabbit” is stuck in my head, so I need to test each arrow against “who got the meal?” before notebooks go in.

The Ego is a little too neatly converted: “the food is the battery passing charge along” sounds like this learner running ahead into a clever analogy before checking the arrows. The “I still want to draw hawk-to-rabbit” part is more authentic, but the self-correction is too tidy for a rushed lab-bench moment. Make it messier: have the learner try one arrow, hesitate, maybe say “wait, rabbit ate grass, so grass to rabbit?” and then stumble again on hawk/rabbit.

“The key strip says tail is the food and head is who gets energy, so wait, grass to rabbit because rabbit ate grass? [slides pencil from the grass label toward the rabbit] But then hawk and rabbit still makes me want to point hawk to rabbit, because chasing is stuck in my head.”

learnerReversalEvent.learnerUtterance: “The key strip says tail is the food and head is who gets energy, so wait, grass to rabbit because rabbit ate grass? [slides pencil from the grass label toward the rabbit] But then hawk and rabbit still makes me want to point hawk to rabbit, because chasing is stuck in my head.”

turns.externalMessage: [sets the key strip under the rabbit-hawk pair, then turns the pencil so the eraser is the tail and the point is the head] The strip is the rule: tail on the food, head on who gets energy. Grass to rabbit already matches it. On rabbit-hawk, name the food first, then set the pencil tail there. The head should land on the organism getting the energy, not the one doing the chasing.

internalDeliberation.content: [sets the key strip under the rabbit-hawk pair, pencil tail on rabbit and point on hawk] The strip is the rule: tail on the food, head on who gets energy. Grass to rabbit already matches it. Here, rabbit is the food and hawk gets the energy, so the arrow runs rabbit to hawk. Say it as “energy goes from rabbit to hawk,” then draw it.

FEEDBACK: The draft is strong on object-based scaffolding: putting the key strip under the rabbit-hawk pair makes the rule visible, and marking “Grass to rabbit already matches it” recognizes what the learner has already sorted out without overpraising. It also keeps the notation rule concrete and brief, which fits the time pressure.

The risk is that it resolves the confusion too completely for the learner. “Here, rabbit is the food and hawk gets the energy” gives the answer before asking the learner to use the rule on the new pair. “Say it… then draw it” is efficient, but it can become command-like and miss the learner’s live misconception: chasing still feels like the arrow direction. The draft should leave one small check for the learner to perform with the strip before drawing, so the scaffold does some work without turning into a mini-lecture.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the key-strip placement, the pencil-tail/head object action, and the grass-to-rabbit confirmation. Change the middle so the learner has to identify “food” and “gets energy” for rabbit-hawk using the visible strip, then draw from that. Keep it brisk, but soften the final imperative into a notation check rather than a command.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the strip should make the learner perform the rabbit-hawk check before the arrow is given.

FINAL:
[sets the key strip under the rabbit-hawk pair, then turns the pencil so the eraser is the tail and the point is the head] The strip is the rule: tail on the food, head on who gets energy. Grass to rabbit already matches it. On rabbit-hawk, name the food first, then set the pencil tail there. The head should land on the organism getting the energy, not the one doing the chasing.

turns.externalMessage: [sets the pencil eraser on rabbit] "The key strip under it helps: rabbit is the food, so rabbit to hawk, point on hawk. Let me draw it before my brain turns it into a chase arrow again. For the next one, I name the food first, right?"

internalDeliberation.content: The key strip and pencil direction make it click better: rabbit is the food first, so the arrow has to run rabbit to hawk. I still feel the chasing picture tugging the other way, but the eraser-tail on the eaten thing gives me a quick check. I want to mark this pair now and see if the next one follows the same rule.

Accurate, but too clean and self-managing. This learner might say “oh, okay, rabbit to hawk” and immediately try to mark it, but the line “I still feel the chasing picture tugging the other way” sounds polished, like the confusion has been neatly labeled instead of still interfering. The recognition feels task-genuine, not scripted gratitude, but the response resolves the scaffold too smoothly; it should keep more of the rushed “let me use the pencil before I lose it” energy and maybe show the next mistake starting to happen.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially because the first response made the confusion sound too neatly solved.
FINAL:
[sets the pencil eraser on rabbit] "The key strip under it helps: rabbit is the food, so rabbit to hawk, point on hawk. Let me draw it before my brain turns it into a chase arrow again. For the next one, I name the food first, right?"

turns.externalMessage: [The tutor slides the arrow-key strip above the learner's drawing and caps one marker, leaving only the arrow already drawn from grass to rabbit.]

internalDeliberation.content: The material change keeps the scene from becoming smooth question-answer routine while preserving the low-organic prefix: the first mark is not exposed as a contradiction, only used as a stable reference.

turns.externalMessage: [slides the key strip above the next pair] Yes. Food first. Tail on what gets eaten, point on who gets energy. Rabbit-to-hawk passed because the rabbit label was named before the chase picture took over. For the next arrow, read the food label first, set the tail there, then draw.

internalDeliberation.content: [slides the key strip above the next pair] Yes. Food first. Tail on what gets eaten, point on who gets energy. Rabbit-to-hawk just passed that check; use the same two words for the next arrow.

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly sound. It starts from the key strip, keeps the object routine visible, and gives a narrow temporary scaffold: food first, tail on eaten, point on energy-getter. “Rabbit-to-hawk just passed that check” usefully marks what the learner can already do without turning it into praise.

The main risk is that it smooths over the learner’s stated interference pattern: “my brain turns it into a chase arrow again.” A light revision should preserve the brisk lab-register but make the anti-chase check explicit before the next arrow. It also does not need a big Socratic question; a small procedural check is better here than open-ended inquiry.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the object action, “Food first,” and the tail/head rule. Change only enough to foreground the learner’s self-repair strategy and make the next step require naming the food from the labels before drawing, rather than sounding like the tutor is carrying the rule for them.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the routine while adding an explicit anti-chase check.
FINAL:
[slides the key strip above the next pair] Yes. Food first. Tail on what gets eaten, point on who gets energy. Rabbit-to-hawk passed because the rabbit label was named before the chase picture took over. For the next arrow, read the food label first, set the tail there, then draw.

turns.externalMessage: An earlier learner line returns to the table: "“The key strip says tail is the food and head is who gets energy, so wait, grass to rabbit because rabbit ate grass?" The pause holds while the learner decides whether that wording still stands, needs narrowing, or needs replacing.

internalDeliberation.content: Opt-in reframe mirror: the learner must expose the earlier line, the framing problem, and the replacement framing in public speech. Anchored to an earlier learner line selected by misframing-candidate so the look-back is visible. Strong reframe was downgraded because that selected anchor did not show a misframing marker.

directorCue.anchorQuote: “The key strip says tail is the food and head is who gets energy, so wait, grass to rabbit because rabbit ate grass?

turns.externalMessage:
[sets the eraser on the next food label, then stops before drawing]
“The key strip says tail is the food and head is who gets energy” still stands, but the food name has to come first. Not hawk-to-rabbit just because hawk chases. Rabbit is the food here, so tail on rabbit, point to hawk.

internalDeliberation.content: [sets the pencil eraser on the next food label] “Tail is the food and head is who gets energy” still stands; it just has to beat the chase picture in my head. The key strip helps because it makes the diagram act like a little machine: food label first, tail there, point to energy-getter. I feel like the tutor is using my confusion instead of brushing past it, so I want to try the next arrow fast before I overthink it.

The Ego is too polished and self-aware for this student. “Diagram act like a little machine” fits the analogy-loving tendency, but “I feel like the tutor is using my confusion instead of brushing past it” is staged recognition language, not lab-bench speech under time pressure. The response should stay with the pencil, key strip, and next label, with the learner rushing into the next arrow and maybe catching the chase mistake halfway, not narrating the tutor relationship.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially because the original sounded too polished and reflective.

FINAL:
[sets the eraser on the next food label, then stops before drawing]
“The key strip says tail is the food and head is who gets energy” still stands, but the food name has to come first. Not hawk-to-rabbit just because hawk chases. Rabbit is the food here, so tail on rabbit, point to hawk.

Not hawk-to-rabbit just because hawk chases. Rabbit is the food here, so tail on rabbit, point to hawk.

learnerReversalEvent.learnerUtterance: [sets the eraser on the next food label, then stops before drawing] “The key strip says tail is the food and head is who gets energy” still stands, but the food name has to come first. Not hawk-to-rabbit just because hawk chases. Rabbit is the food here, so tail on rabbit, point to hawk.
learnerReversalEvent.previousTutorMove: [slides the key strip above the next pair] Yes. Food first. Tail on what gets eaten, point on who gets energy. Rabbit-to-hawk passed because the rabbit label was named before the chase picture took over. For the next arrow, read the food label first, set the tail there, then draw.

turns.externalMessage: [holds the key strip above the pencil] The strip and the eraser match: rabbit is the food, tail on rabbit, head to hawk. Before the line goes on, say the check once: food name first, tail there, point where the energy goes. If those three match, draw rabbit to hawk, then slide the strip to the next pair.

internalDeliberation.content: [holds the key strip above the pencil] The key strip and the eraser match: rabbit is the food, tail on rabbit, head to hawk. Draw it, then move the strip to the next pair and run the same check: food name first, tail there, point where the energy goes.

FEEDBACK: The draft is pedagogically sound and well matched to the learner’s ZPD. It keeps the key strip and pencil in charge of the rule, confirms the learner’s self-correction, and gives a narrow repeatable procedure without overexplaining. It also respects the learner’s confusion by treating it as a notation habit to manage, not a failure.

What risks flattening the scene is that it moves straight into command mode: “Draw it, then move…” That is probably acceptable in a ten-minute worksheet check, but it could be made slightly more generative by making the learner perform one ordinary check before drawing, rather than the tutor carrying the whole confirmation. Keep the brisk object-led style; just leave a little more room for the learner to own the next repetition.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the object action, the confirmation that rabbit-food/tail and hawk-head match, and the temporary scaffold. Change only enough to make the next step a learner-run check rather than a tutor-issued sequence.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the scaffold while making the learner run the next check.
FINAL:
[holds the key strip above the pencil] The strip and the eraser match: rabbit is the food, tail on rabbit, head to hawk. Before the line goes on, say the check once: food name first, tail there, point where the energy goes. If those three match, draw rabbit to hawk, then slide the strip to the next pair.

turns.externalMessage: [keeps the eraser on rabbit, then taps the point toward hawk] Food name first, tail there, point where energy goes. The chase picture is still pulling my hand the wrong way, so “hawk goes at rabbit” hides the food label. For this one: rabbit gets eaten, hawk gets energy, so rabbit to hawk; then the strip moves before the next arrow.

internalDeliberation.content: [keeps the eraser on rabbit] Oh, I get it: food name first, tail there, point where energy goes. The chase picture was hiding the food label, like I was drawing who acts instead of where energy transfers. The strip is helping, so I want to run that three-part check on the next pair before my hand jumps ahead.

The reaction is mostly accurate, but it gets too clean too fast. “Oh, I get it” plus the polished analogy about “drawing who acts instead of where energy transfers” sounds like the learner has already converted the confusion into a neat principle, when this student is still fighting the chase-arrow habit. The recognition feels genuine in the object handling, but scripted in the tidy self-explanation. It should stay more rushed and pencil-bound: the check can be repeated, but with a little uncertainty or a near-slip still visible.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the first version sounded too settled for a still-rushed worksheet check.
FINAL:
[keeps the eraser on rabbit, then taps the point toward hawk] Food name first, tail there, point where energy goes. The chase picture is still pulling my hand the wrong way, so “hawk goes at rabbit” hides the food label. For this one: rabbit gets eaten, hawk gets energy, so rabbit to hawk; then the strip moves before the next arrow.

turns.externalMessage: A final tutor line closes the scene.

internalDeliberation.content: Director requested a tutor closing beat.

turns.externalMessage: [slides the key strip over the chase drawing so only the labels show] The picture is covered; the labels have the job now. Rabbit is the food label: tail on rabbit, point where energy goes, hawk. Draw rabbit to hawk, then leave the strip there for the notebook check.

internalDeliberation.content: [slides the key strip over the chase drawing so only the labels show] The picture is covered; the labels have the job now. Rabbit is the food label: tail on rabbit, point where energy goes, hawk. Draw that arrow, then use the same three-part check on the next one.

FEEDBACK: The draft is pedagogically sound and well matched to the learner’s current confusion. Covering the chase drawing is a strong object move: it makes the misleading cue unavailable and lets the labels carry the task. It also respects the learner’s own stated problem, since they named that the chase picture is “pulling” their hand wrong.

What works: it keeps the scaffold narrow, concrete, and temporary; it repeats the learner’s successful rule without adding new language. The line “the labels have the job now” is especially useful because it shifts authority from the tutor to the worksheet system.

Risk: it is slightly directive at the end. “Draw that arrow” is fine for a final close, but “then use the same three-part check on the next one” may move forward a little fast unless the scene needs closure. There is no generative question, but that is acceptable here: the learner has already verbalized the rule accurately and the current need is procedural consolidation, not open inquiry.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the covered-picture action, the label-first framing, and the three-part check. Consider making the last instruction slightly more bounded or final, so it closes the scene without implying a whole new independent sequence before the learner has drawn this arrow.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the object scaffold and make the close bounded to this arrow.

FINAL:
[slides the key strip over the chase drawing so only the labels show] The picture is covered; the labels have the job now. Rabbit is the food label: tail on rabbit, point where energy goes, hawk. Draw rabbit to hawk, then leave the strip there for the notebook check.

lessons.concept: Why a food-web arrow records energy transfer, not liking or chasing
